entities.description: First human and first prophet, created by Allah from clay

Built the Ark by Allah's command, survived the Great Flood
Sent to the people of 'Ad
Sent to the people of Thamud with the she-camel as a sign
Father of monotheism, builder of the Kaaba with his son Ismail
Nephew of Ibrahim, sent to the people of Sodom
Son of Ibrahim, helped build the Kaaba, ancestor of Prophet Muhammad
Son of Ibrahim and Sarah, father of Yaqub
Son of Ishaq, also known as Israel, father of the twelve tribes
Son of Yaqub, given the gift of dream interpretation, became ruler of Egypt
Known for extraordinary patience through severe trials
Sent to the people of Madyan, preached honest trade
Received the Torah, led the Israelites out of Egypt, spoke directly to Allah
Brother of Musa, appointed as his helper and minister
King and prophet, given the Zabur (Psalms), defeated Goliath
Son of Dawud, given dominion over jinn, animals, and wind
Called his people away from idol worship of Baal
Successor of Ilyas, mentioned among the righteous
Swallowed by a whale, his people repented and were saved
Guardian of Maryam, father of Yahya, prayed for a son in old age
Son of Zakariyya, confirmed the word of Allah
Born of Maryam, performed miracles by Allah's permission, given the Injeel
The final prophet and messenger, received the Quran
Mentioned among the patient and righteous prophets
Mother of Isa, chosen above all women of the worlds
Wise man who gave profound advice to his son
Righteous ruler who traveled to the ends of the earth and built a barrier against Yajuj and Majuj
Tyrant ruler of Egypt who opposed Musa, drowned in the Red Sea
Minister of Pharaoh who built the tower
Wealthy Israelite who was arrogant and swallowed by the earth
Giant warrior defeated by Dawud
King appointed by Allah to lead the Israelites against Jalut
Figure claimed by some as son of God, rebuked in the Quran
Mysterious servant of Allah whom Musa traveled with to learn hidden wisdom
Refused to prostrate to Adam, vowed to mislead humanity
Queen who accepted faith after encountering Sulayman's wisdom
Righteous woman who believed despite Pharaoh, example for believers
Young believers who slept in a cave for centuries, protected by Allah

Angel responsible for provisions and sustenance

Guardian angel of Hellfire
Two angels sent to Babylon as a test, taught magic as a trial

Angels who question the dead in their graves
Sacred city, location of the Kaaba, birthplace of Prophet Muhammad
City of the Prophet, destination of the Hijra
Sacred House in Mecca, first house of worship built for mankind
Mountain where Musa received the Torah and spoke to Allah

Valley where a battle took place after the conquest of Mecca
Plain where pilgrims stand during Hajj
Two hills between which Hajar ran seeking water, now part of Hajj rites

City of Prophet Shuayb, near the Gulf of Aqaba
Ancient city where Harut and Marut were sent
The eternal garden of reward for the righteous
The abode of punishment for the disbelievers and evildoers
The farthest mosque, destination of the Night Journey
Lost city of the people of 'Ad
Dwelling place of the people of Thamud
Settlement near Madyan, associated with Prophet Shuayb
Ancient kingdom of Yemen mentioned as a destroyed civilization
People mentioned among the destroyed nations
The all-encompassing mercy of Allah that covers all creation
The special mercy of Allah reserved for the believers
The absolute ruler and king of all existence
The pure and perfect, free from all deficiency
The one free from all defects, the source of peace and safety
The guarantor of security and faith
The watcher over all things
The one who is mighty and powerful, prevailing over all
The irresistible one who compels all to His will
The one who is above all greatness
The one who brings everything into existence from nothing
The one who creates with no precedent
The one who shapes all forms
The one who forgives abundantly and repeatedly
The one who dominates all and is never overcome
The one who gives generously without return
The one who provides sustenance to all creation
The one whose knowledge encompasses all things
The one who hears all things
The one who sees all things
The final arbiter who judges with absolute justice
The one who is absolutely just in all decrees
The gentle and subtle one, aware of the finest details
The one who is aware of the inner nature of all things
The one who is patient and delays punishment
The immensely great, whose greatness is beyond measure
The one who forgives completely and abundantly
The one who rewards even the smallest good deed abundantly
The one who is above all creation in every way
The one who is greater than everything
The one who guards and preserves all things
The one who sustains and nourishes all things
The one who takes account of all things
The one full of loving kindness
The one whose every decree contains perfect wisdom
The one who has no beginning or end to His life
The one who sustains all existence by Himself
The light of the heavens and the earth
The one who is before everything
The one who remains after all else has perished
The one whose existence is evident from all signs
The one whose essence is beyond perception
The one who continually accepts repentance
The one who pardons and erases sins completely
The one full of compassion and kindness
The one with absolute and perfect power over all things
The one upon whom all depend, who depends on none
The uniquely one, without partner or equal
The absolutely unique, indivisible one
The protective friend and ally of the believers
The oneness of Allah — the central and most fundamental theme of the Quran
Obligatory and voluntary prayers, the pillar of Islam
Endurance, perseverance, and patience in the face of trials
Thankfulness to Allah for His countless blessings
Turning back to Allah in sincere repentance
Reliance and dependence on Allah in all matters
Establishing justice and equity in all dealings
Allah's all-encompassing mercy and the call to be merciful
Obligatory and voluntary giving to purify wealth and help the needy
The final day when all will be judged and receive their recompense
Belief in Allah, His angels, books, messengers, the Last Day, and divine decree
The danger of hypocrites who outwardly profess faith but conceal disbelief
Constant remembrance and mention of Allah
Consciousness of Allah, piety, and mindfulness of one's duties
Spiritual excellence, worshipping Allah as if seeing Him
The pursuit of knowledge and its virtue in Islam
Obligatory fasting in Ramadan and voluntary fasting
The annual pilgrimage to Mecca, a pillar of Islam
Striving and struggling for the sake of Allah, including self-improvement and defense
Inviting people to the path of Allah with wisdom and good counsel
Allah's predestination and the wisdom behind divine decree
Kindness, respect, and dutiful care toward parents
The reality of death and what comes after
Allah as the sole provider and the test of wealth and poverty
Mutual consultation in decision-making
Fulfilling trusts and being trustworthy
Tests and trials as a means of purification
Asking Allah for forgiveness as an act of worship
Noble character traits including honesty, generosity, humility, and kindness
Calling upon Allah directly in prayer and supplication
Natural phenomena as signs pointing to Allah's existence and power
Prohibition of usury and interest-based transactions
Marriage laws, rights of spouses, and family life
Laws of inheritance and distribution of estate
Lawful and unlawful food and drink
Modesty in dress and behavior for men and women
Unity and brotherhood of the Muslim community
The duty to promote virtue and prevent vice
entities.name_urdu: امر بالمعروف و نہی عن المنکر

entities.description: Self-referential passages about the Quran's nature, purpose, and inimitability
Allah created Adam from clay and commanded the angels to prostrate before him
Adam and Hawwa ate from the forbidden tree and were sent to earth

entities.name_english: Story of Cain and Abel (Habil & Qabil)
entities.description: The first murder — Qabil killed his brother Habil out of jealousy
Nuh's flood that destroyed the disbelievers, only those on the Ark survived
Ibrahim's people threw him into a great fire, but Allah made it cool and safe
entities.name_urdu: ابراہیم علیہ السلام کا آگ میں ڈالا جانا

entities.description: Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son, and Allah's substitution of a ram
Ibrahim and Ismail raised the foundations of the Sacred House
Allah rained stones upon the people of Lut for their transgression
The mighty people of 'Ad destroyed by a furious wind for rejecting Hud
Thamud destroyed by a blast after hamstringing the she-camel
The comprehensive story of Yusuf: dream, betrayal, prison, rise to power, family reunion
Musa led the Israelites out of Egypt, the sea was split, Pharaoh drowned
The Israelites worshipped a golden calf while Musa was on Mount Sinai
Musa's journey with Al-Khidr to learn the hidden wisdom behind seemingly unjust events
Young believers who fled persecution and slept in a cave for 309 years
Prophet Muhammad's miraculous night journey from Mecca to Jerusalem and ascension to the heavens
First major battle of Islam where 313 Muslims defeated over 1000 Quraysh
Second major battle where Muslims suffered setback due to archers leaving their posts
The confederates besieged Medina; Allah sent wind and forces to scatter them
The peaceful conquest of Mecca by the Muslims
Peace treaty that became a clear victory for the Muslims
Abraha's army with elephants was destroyed by birds when attacking the Kaaba
The first verses revealed to Prophet Muhammad in the Cave of Hira
Prophet Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina
False accusation against Aisha (RA), cleared by divine revelation
Young Dawud defeated the giant warrior Jalut in battle
entities.name_urdu: داؤد کا جالوت کو شکست دینا

entities.name_english: Sulayman and the Queen of Sheba
entities.description: The hoopoe brought news of Sheba, and its queen eventually accepted faith
Yunus was swallowed by a whale after leaving his people, repented in its belly
Maryam gave birth to Isa under a palm tree without a father
Isa's disciples asked for a table of food from heaven as a sign
Destructive peoples sealed behind a barrier by Dhul-Qarnayn, will break free before the Last Day
Israfil will blow the trumpet signaling the end of the world and resurrection
All humans will be resurrected from their graves for judgment
Each soul will be given their record and judged for their deeds
All people will cross a bridge over Hellfire on the Day of Judgment
The dam of Ma'rib broke, destroying the civilization of Saba
The five daily prayers, their times, and conditions
Mandatory charity as percentage of wealth, categories of recipients
Rules and exemptions for Ramadan fasting
Pilgrimage rituals, obligations, and prohibitions during ihram
Ritual purification before prayer, including dry ablution
Prohibition of pork, blood, carrion, and improperly slaughtered animals
Progressive prohibition of intoxicants and gambling
Detailed shares for heirs including parents, spouses, and children
Rules of marriage including mahr, consent, and prohibited relationships
Rules governing divorce, iddah (waiting period), and reconciliation
Rules for bearing witness and the importance of truthful testimony
Laws of just retribution for murder and bodily harm, with option to forgive
Fixed punishments for theft, adultery, and false accusation
Absolute prohibition of interest-based lending and borrowing
Writing down debts, fulfilling contracts, and fair trade
When fighting is permitted, treatment of prisoners, peace treaties
Rulings on making, breaking, and atoning for oaths
Lowering the gaze, covering, and rules of modest dress
Voluntary giving, its virtue, and etiquette (not followed by reminders)
Atonement for broken oaths, accidental killing, or zihar
Mighty ancient civilization destroyed for rejecting Prophet Hud
Rock-dwelling civilization destroyed for rejecting Prophet Salih
The Israelites — their covenant, blessings, trials, and transgressions
The Prophet Muhammad's tribe in Mecca
Prosperous civilization of Yemen blessed then punished for ingratitude
Uncle of the Prophet who opposed Islam, condemned in the Quran

entities.name_english: Creation of the Heavens and Earth
entities.description: Allah created the heavens and earth in six periods
Allah created jinn from smokeless fire before humans
Allah took a covenant from all souls of Adam's descendants before creation

The wife of the Egyptian minister Al-Aziz who attempted to seduce the prophet Yusuf and, when he refused, falsely accused him of impropriety leading to his imprisonment. She later publicly acknowledged her wrongdoing and Yusuf's innocence. Her story is a central thread in the narrative of Surah Yusuf.

A righteous believer who lived as the wife of one of history's greatest tyrants yet maintained unwavering faith in Allah. She rescued and raised the infant Musa, and the Quran presents her as one of the best examples for all believers. Her prayer for a house in Paradise near Allah is recorded in Surah At-Tahrim.

The wife of Prophet Lut who betrayed her husband and remained loyal to the corrupt people of Sodom. The Quran uses her as a warning example of one who squanders the privilege of living with a prophet by choosing treachery. She perished along with the people of Sodom.

The wife of Prophet Nuh who refused to believe despite living with a messenger of Allah for centuries. The Quran presents her alongside the wife of Lut as an example that family ties do not guarantee salvation without personal faith. She was among those who drowned in the flood.

The man who misled the Israelites during Musa's absence on Mount Sinai by crafting a golden calf from their jewelry and ornaments, which produced a lowing sound. He was confronted by Musa and banished, condemned to spend his life in isolation. His story is a cautionary tale about those who exploit crises to spread deviation.

The holiest city in Islam, located in present-day Saudi Arabia and home to the Sacred Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) and the Ka'bah. It is the birthplace of Prophet Muhammad and the destination of the annual Hajj pilgrimage. The city is also referred to as Bakkah and Umm al-Qura in the Quran.

The second holiest city in Islam, to which the Prophet Muhammad migrated from Makkah in the Hijra. It became the center of the early Muslim community and state, and contains the Prophet's Mosque (Masjid an-Nabawi). The Quran references it in connection with the hypocrites and the battles fought nearby.

The ancient land of the Nile referenced in the Quran as the setting for the trials and triumphs of the prophets Yusuf and Musa. It was the land of Pharaoh's tyranny as well as Yusuf's rise to power as a minister. Egypt represents both the heights of civilization and the depths of injustice in Quranic narratives.

The sacred mountain where Allah spoke directly to Prophet Musa and gave him the Torah. The Quran swears by it as a sign of divine majesty and references it as the site of the great covenant with the Israelites. It is known in the Quran as Al-Tur and is among the most sacred places in the Abrahamic tradition.

The cubic structure at the center of Masjid al-Haram in Makkah, considered the House of Allah and the holiest site in Islam. It was built by Ibrahim and his son Ismail as the first house of worship dedicated to the one God. Muslims face it in prayer and circumambulate it during Hajj and Umrah.

The farthest mosque, located in Jerusalem, which was the destination of the Prophet's miraculous Night Journey. It is considered the third holiest site in Islam and was the first qibla (direction of prayer) for Muslims. The surrounding land is described in the Quran as blessed.

The cave where a group of young believers sought refuge from religious persecution and were put to sleep by Allah for an extended period of 309 years. It serves as a symbol of divine protection for those who flee oppression for the sake of their faith. Surah Al-Kahf takes its name from this cave.

The act of sincerely turning back to Allah after committing sin, involving remorse, cessation of the sin, and firm resolve not to repeat it. The Quran emphasizes that Allah loves those who repent and that His door of forgiveness is always open. Tawbah is a fundamental concept in Islamic spirituality and moral reform.

The Islamic principle of mutual consultation in decision-making, enjoined upon the Prophet and the believers. The Quran commands the Prophet to consult his companions in affairs and describes believers as those who conduct their matters by mutual consultation. Shura is foundational to Islamic governance and community leadership.

The highest level of worship and moral conduct, defined as worshipping Allah as though you see Him, or knowing that He sees you. Beyond mere compliance with rules, ihsan represents the internalization of faith into beauty of character and action. The Quran frequently promises that Allah is with those who practice ihsan.

Comprehensive righteousness encompassing faith, charity, prayer, patience, and fulfilling commitments. The Quran provides a detailed definition of birr in Surah Al-Baqarah, distinguishing true righteousness from merely following outward rituals. It represents the wholeness of Islamic virtue in belief, worship, and conduct.

The concept of moral, social, and spiritual corruption that the Quran repeatedly condemns and warns against. It includes injustice, transgression, dishonesty, and the spreading of disorder in society. The Quran states that Allah does not love those who cause fasad and that it brings destruction to individuals and communities.

The innate, God-given nature of the human being that is inclined toward recognizing and worshipping Allah. The Quran references fitrah in Surah Ar-Rum in the context of the natural religion of Islam, stating it as the pure original disposition. Every child is born upon this fitrah before external influences shape their beliefs.

The doctrine that Allah has decreed all things that occur in creation, past, present, and future, in accordance with His perfect knowledge and wisdom. Belief in qadr is one of the six pillars of Islamic faith and encompasses the understanding that both good and hardship come from Allah. The Quran repeatedly affirms that nothing occurs except by Allah's permission and decree.

The quality of being ever-mindful of Allah, guarding oneself against disobedience, and living with a constant awareness of accountability before Him. The Quran identifies taqwa as the criterion of honor before Allah and repeatedly commands believers to attain it. It is described as the best provision for the journey of life and the hereafter.

The foundational Islamic belief in the absolute oneness and uniqueness of Allah, with no partners, equals, or intermediaries. Tawhid encompasses the oneness of Allah's lordship, worship, and divine attributes. It is the central message of every prophet and the defining characteristic of Islam.

The gravest sin in Islam, defined as attributing partners, equals, or associates to Allah in worship, lordship, or His attributes. The Quran states that Allah may forgive all sins except shirk if a person dies without repenting from it. It encompasses open idolatry as well as subtle forms such as showing off in worship.

The Quranic imperative to establish justice in all affairs, from personal dealings to societal governance. Allah commands justice as one of His core commandments alongside kindness and giving to relatives. Justice is both an attribute of Allah and an obligation placed upon believers in their conduct with all people.

The trust or moral responsibility that Allah offered to the heavens, earth, and mountains, but which only the human being accepted. In everyday life it encompasses fulfilling commitments, safeguarding what is entrusted to one, and being honest in all dealings. The Quran presents fulfillment of amanah as a key characteristic of the true believers.

The belief that all sustenance — food, wealth, health, and every blessing — comes ultimately from Allah the Provider. The Quran commands believers to seek rizq through lawful means while trusting entirely in Allah's guarantee to provide for all living things. It is a concept that encompasses material provision as well as spiritual and moral nourishment.

The constant remembrance, mention, and contemplation of Allah through words, heart, and action. The Quran declares that it is in the remembrance of Allah that hearts find rest, and it enjoins believers to remember Allah often. Dhikr ranges from ritual phrases to mindful awareness of Allah's presence in every moment.

The miraculous night journey of Prophet Muhammad from Masjid al-Haram in Makkah to Masjid al-Aqsa in Jerusalem, followed by his ascension through the heavens. During the ascension he was given the five daily prayers and encountered the previous prophets. It is one of the greatest miracles of the Prophet and is referenced in Surahs Al-Isra and An-Najm.

The largely peaceful entry of the Muslim forces into Makkah in 8 AH, when the Prophet Muhammad granted general amnesty to its inhabitants. The conquest fulfilled the promise of Allah and cleared the Sacred Mosque of idols. It is referenced in Surah Al-Fath as a clear victory and anticipated in Surah An-Nasr.

The final day when all creation will be resurrected and gathered before Allah for the ultimate judgment. The Quran describes it with many names — the Day of Gathering, the Hour, the Day of Reckoning — and depicts it with vivid imagery of cosmic upheaval and the weighing of deeds. It is one of the most frequently referenced events in the Quran, appearing in over fifty surahs.

The primordial covenant described in Surah Al-A'raf where Allah drew forth all the souls of Adam's descendants and asked them: 'Am I not your Lord?' and all replied: 'Yes, we testify.' This covenant is the foundation of the human soul's innate recognition of Allah. It is invoked in the Quran as evidence against those who will claim ignorance on the Day of Judgment.

Destructive peoples whose emergence is mentioned as one of the major signs before the Day of Judgment. They were contained behind a great barrier built by Dhul-Qarnayn and will break free when the time ordained by Allah arrives. Their release is described in both Surah Al-Kahf and Surah Al-Anbiya as a sign of the approaching end of time.

A group of young believers who fled their pagan society to protect their faith and took refuge in a cave, where Allah put them into a miraculous sleep for 309 years. Their story, told in detail in Surah Al-Kahf, demonstrates that Allah protects those who stand firm in their faith. They are held up as a sign of divine power over time and death.

The army led by Abraha, the Abyssinian governor of Yemen, who marched on Makkah with war elephants intending to destroy the Ka'bah in the Year of the Elephant, the birth year of Prophet Muhammad. Allah destroyed the army by sending flocks of birds that pelted them with stones of baked clay. This event is commemorated in Surah Al-Fil.

Two angels mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah who were sent to Babylon as a trial and test for the people. They taught the knowledge of magic only after warning that doing so was disbelief, and they informed people that this knowledge would be used to harm rather than benefit. The people's misuse of this knowledge is cited as an example of how they chose what harmed them over what benefited them.

The dominant tribe of Makkah and the tribe of Prophet Muhammad, who were the custodians of the Ka'bah and the most powerful mercantile clan of Arabia. Surah Quraysh reminds them of Allah's favors in providing them safety for their winter and summer trade journeys and commands them to worship the Lord of the Ka'bah. They were initially the primary opponents of the Prophet and the early Muslim community.
The Earth, created by Allah as the dwelling place of humanity. Referenced throughout the Quran in relation to creation, stewardship (khilafah), and signs of divine power.
